Deities-eng.svg|frame]] {{ill|Omodaru and Ayakashikone|ja|オモダル・アヤカシコネ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omodaru and Ayakashikone}} is a pair of Japanese primordial deities<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Aragona |first=Jared |title=from Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Vol. 1 |url=https://open.maricopa.edu/worldmythologyvolume1godsandcreation/chapter/from-nihongi-chronicles-of-japan-from-the-earliest-times-to-a-d-697-vol-1/ |language=en}}</ref><ref name="heavens">{{Cite web |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 1 |url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697/Book_I |via=Wikisource}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=2021-04-11 |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Omodaru, Ayakashikone |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=124 |access-date=2023-03-22 |archive-url=http://web.archive.org/web/20210411072506/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=124 |archive-date=2021-04-11 }}</ref><ref>https://archive.ph/a8w1z</ref>
They were preceded by {{ill|Otonoji and Otonobe|ja|オオトノヂ・オオトノベ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otonoji and Otonobe|qq=}} and succeeded by [[Izanagi]] and {{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami|1=Izanami}}.
In medieval times they were identified with the {{ill|Demon King of the Sixth Heaven|lt=Demon King of the Sixth Heaven|ja|天魔|lt_ja=第六天魔王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Demon King of the Sixth Heaven}} <ref>https://web.archive.org/web/20221225101550/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9171</ref>
==Overview== [[Image:Japanese Primordial Deities-ja.svg|thumb|right|200px|{{ill|Creation of Heaven and Earth|ja|天地開闢|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Creation of Heaven and Earth|1=Q11442446|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11442446}} {{ill|Japanese Primordial Deities (SVG)|ja|メディア:Japanese Primordial Deities-ja.svg|lt=SVG display (compatible browsers only)|lt_ja=SVGで表示（対応ブラウザのみ）|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Primordial Deities (SVG)|qq=}}]]
'''Omotaru''' (淤母陀琉, also known as Menashison) ・ '''Ayakashikone'''／'''Ayakashikine''' (阿夜訶志古泥, also known as Kyoukonson) are deities appearing in {{ill|Japanese mythology|en|Japanese mythology|WD=Q814158|lt=Japanese Mythology|1=Japanese mythology}}. {{Needs verification|{{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Jinmu Tennō|1=Emperor Jimmu}} is his great-grandson|date=February 2025}}
== Overview == In the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki|1=Kojiki}}, the elder sibling is presented as '''Omotaru-no-Kami''' and the younger as '''Ayakashikone-no-Kami'''; in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}, the elder is rendered as '''Menashison''' and the younger as '''Ayakashikone''' (Ayakashikine).
In the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki|1=Kojiki}}, they are regarded as the sixth-generation deities of the {{ill|Kamiyonanayo|en|Kamiyonanayo|WD=Q3275893|lt=Kamiyonanayo|1=Kamiyonanayo}}, with the elder, Omotaru-no-Kami, being a male deity and the younger, Ayakashikone-no-Kami, a female deity. The name "Omotaru" means "completed (i.e., without deficiency)," while "Ayakashikone" is an honorific epithet derived from "ayanikashi"—thus, they deify the perfection of the human body. {{Sfn|Kurano|2007|p=20}}{{Sfn|Nakayama|1999|p=59}}
Furthermore, Omotaru-no-Kami is interpreted by reading "淤母" as "face" and "陀琉" as "to suffice," thereby naming him to denote "a man whose facial appearance is completely satisfying." In light of the contextual correspondence with Ayakashikone-no-Kami and the enduring practice of phallic worship, his naming is also seen as a praise of the male genital form. Similarly, Ayakashikone-no-Kami is parsed with "阿夜" as an interjection, "訶志古" as the stem of "to fear," and "泥" as an honorific for persons; hence, her name implies "Oh, most venerable woman," symbolizing the awe inspired by the potent spiritual power of the female genitalia. {{Sfn|Nishimiya|2014|pp=325–326}}
In the {{ill|Middle Ages|en|Middle Ages|WD=Q12554|lt=Medieval Period|1=Middle Ages}}, through the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism ({{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|en|Shinbutsu-shūgō|WD=Q2279088|lt=Shinbutsu-shūgō|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}}), these deities—as the sixth-generation in the {{ill|Kamiyonanayo|en|Kamiyonanayo|WD=Q3275893|lt=Kamiyonanayo|1=Kamiyonanayo}}—came to be regarded as the manifestation ({{ill|Suijaku|ja|垂迹|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suijaku|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 本地垂迹|1=Honji suijaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3080781}}) of the {{ill|Demon King of the Sixth Heaven|lt=Sixth Heavenly Demon King|ja|天魔|1=Demon King of the Sixth Heaven|lt=Demon King of the Sixth Heaven|1=Demon King of the Sixth Heaven|qq=Sixth Heavenly Demon King|lt_ja=第六天魔王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Demon King of the Sixth Heaven|13=User:Immanuelle/Demon King of the Sixth Heaven|qq=}}, the highest Rank of the {{ill|six heavens of the desire realm|ja|六欲天|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/six heavens of the desire realm|qq=}} in the Buddhist desire realm. They were especially venerated in {{ill|Shugendō|en|Shugendō|WD=Q1483875|lt=Shugendō|1=Shugendō}}. With the separation of Shinto and Buddhism in the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} ({{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|WD=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu Bunri|1=Shinbutsu bunri}}), many temples ({{ill|Buddhist Temple|ja|寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhist Temple|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 寺院|1=Buddhist temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5393308}}) enshrining the Sixth Heavenly Demon King were converted into shrines ({{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto Shrine|1=Shinto shrine}}), and were renamed as, for example, {{ill|Dairokuten Shrine|lt=Dairokuten Shrines|ja|第六天神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dairokuten Shrine|qq=}}, Koro Shrine, and {{ill|Menashi Shrine|ja|面足神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Menashi Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 第六天神社|1=Dairokuten Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11602399}}.

Deities-eng.svg|frame]] {{ill|Omodaru and Ayakashikone|ja|オモダル・アヤカシコネ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omodaru and Ayakashikone}} is a pair of Japanese primordial deities<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Aragona |first=Jared |title=from Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Vol. 1 |url=https://open.maricopa.edu/worldmythologyvolume1godsandcreation/chapter/from-nihongi-chronicles-of-japan-from-the-earliest-times-to-a-d-697-vol-1/ |language=en}}</ref><ref name="heavens">{{Cite web |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 1 |url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697/Book_I |via=Wikisource}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=2021-04-11 |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Omodaru, Ayakashikone |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=124 |access-date=2023-03-22 |archive-url=http://web.archive.org/web/20210411072506/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=124 |archive-date=2021-04-11 }}</ref><ref>https://archive.ph/a8w1z</ref>
They were preceded by {{ill|Otonoji and Otonobe|ja|オオトノヂ・オオトノベ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otonoji and Otonobe|qq=}} and succeeded by [[Izanagi]] and {{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami|1=Izanami}}.
In medieval times they were identified with the {{ill|Demon King of the Sixth Heaven|lt=Demon King of the Sixth Heaven|ja|天魔|lt_ja=第六天魔王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Demon King of the Sixth Heaven}} <ref>https://web.archive.org/web/20221225101550/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9171</ref>
==Overview== [[Image:Japanese Primordial Deities-ja.svg|thumb|right|200px|{{ill|Creation of Heaven and Earth|ja|天地開闢|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Creation of Heaven and Earth|1=Q11442446|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11442446}} {{ill|Japanese Primordial Deities (SVG)|ja|メディア:Japanese Primordial Deities-ja.svg|lt=SVG display (compatible browsers only)|lt_ja=SVGで表示（対応ブラウザのみ）|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Primordial Deities (SVG)|qq=}}]]
'''Omotaru''' (淤母陀琉, also known as Menashison) ・ '''Ayakashikone'''／'''Ayakashikine''' (阿夜訶志古泥, also known as Kyoukonson) are deities appearing in {{ill|Japanese mythology|en|Japanese mythology|WD=Q814158|lt=Japanese Mythology|1=Japanese mythology}}. {{Needs verification|{{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Jinmu Tennō|1=Emperor Jimmu}} is his great-grandson|date=February 2025}}
== Overview == In the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki|1=Kojiki}}, the elder sibling is presented as '''Omotaru-no-Kami''' and the younger as '''Ayakashikone-no-Kami'''; in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}, the elder is rendered as '''Menashison''' and the younger as '''Ayakashikone''' (Ayakashikine).
In the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki|1=Kojiki}}, they are regarded as the sixth-generation deities of the {{ill|Kamiyonanayo|en|Kamiyonanayo|WD=Q3275893|lt=Kamiyonanayo|1=Kamiyonanayo}}, with the elder, Omotaru-no-Kami, being a male deity and the younger, Ayakashikone-no-Kami, a female deity. The name "Omotaru" means "completed (i.e., without deficiency)," while "Ayakashikone" is an honorific epithet derived from "ayanikashi"—thus, they deify the perfection of the human body. {{Sfn|Kurano|2007|p=20}}{{Sfn|Nakayama|1999|p=59}}
Furthermore, Omotaru-no-Kami is interpreted by reading "淤母" as "face" and "陀琉" as "to suffice," thereby naming him to denote "a man whose facial appearance is completely satisfying." In light of the contextual correspondence with Ayakashikone-no-Kami and the enduring practice of phallic worship, his naming is also seen as a praise of the male genital form. Similarly, Ayakashikone-no-Kami is parsed with "阿夜" as an interjection, "訶志古" as the stem of "to fear," and "泥" as an honorific for persons; hence, her name implies "Oh, most venerable woman," symbolizing the awe inspired by the potent spiritual power of the female genitalia. {{Sfn|Nishimiya|2014|pp=325–326}}
In the {{ill|Middle Ages|en|Middle Ages|WD=Q12554|lt=Medieval Period|1=Middle Ages}}, through the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism ({{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|en|Shinbutsu-shūgō|WD=Q2279088|lt=Shinbutsu-shūgō|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}}), these deities—as the sixth-generation in the {{ill|Kamiyonanayo|en|Kamiyonanayo|WD=Q3275893|lt=Kamiyonanayo|1=Kamiyonanayo}}—came to be regarded as the manifestation ({{ill|Suijaku|ja|垂迹|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suijaku|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 本地垂迹|1=Honji suijaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3080781}}) of the {{ill|Demon King of the Sixth Heaven|lt=Sixth Heavenly Demon King|ja|天魔|1=Demon King of the Sixth Heaven|lt=Demon King of the Sixth Heaven|1=Demon King of the Sixth Heaven|qq=Sixth Heavenly Demon King|lt_ja=第六天魔王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Demon King of the Sixth Heaven|13=User:Immanuelle/Demon King of the Sixth Heaven|qq=}}, the highest Rank of the {{ill|six heavens of the desire realm|ja|六欲天|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/six heavens of the desire realm|qq=}} in the Buddhist desire realm. They were especially venerated in {{ill|Shugendō|en|Shugendō|WD=Q1483875|lt=Shugendō|1=Shugendō}}. With the separation of Shinto and Buddhism in the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} ({{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|WD=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu Bunri|1=Shinbutsu bunri}}), many temples ({{ill|Buddhist Temple|ja|寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhist Temple|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 寺院|1=Buddhist temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5393308}}) enshrining the Sixth Heavenly Demon King were converted into shrines ({{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto Shrine|1=Shinto shrine}}), and were renamed as, for example, {{ill|Dairokuten Shrine|lt=Dairokuten Shrines|ja|第六天神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dairokuten Shrine|qq=}}, Koro Shrine, and {{ill|Menashi Shrine|ja|面足神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Menashi Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 第六天神社|1=Dairokuten Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11602399}}.

Deities-eng.svg|frame]] {{ill|Omodaru and Ayakashikone|ja|オモダル・アヤカシコネ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omodaru and Ayakashikone}} is a pair of Japanese primordial deities<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Aragona |first=Jared |title=from Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Vol. 1 |url=https://open.maricopa.edu/worldmythologyvolume1godsandcreation/chapter/from-nihongi-chronicles-of-japan-from-the-earliest-times-to-a-d-697-vol-1/ |language=en}}</ref><ref name="heavens">{{Cite web |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 1 |url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697/Book_I |via=Wikisource}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=2021-04-11 |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Omodaru, Ayakashikone |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=124 |access-date=2023-03-22 |archive-url=http://web.archive.org/web/20210411072506/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=124 |archive-date=2021-04-11 }}</ref><ref>https://archive.ph/a8w1z</ref>
They were preceded by {{ill|Otonoji and Otonobe|ja|オオトノヂ・オオトノベ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otonoji and Otonobe|qq=}} and succeeded by [[Izanagi]] and {{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami|1=Izanami}}.
In medieval times they were identified with the {{ill|Demon King of the Sixth Heaven|lt=Demon King of the Sixth Heaven|ja|天魔|lt_ja=第六天魔王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Demon King of the Sixth Heaven}} <ref>https://web.archive.org/web/20221225101550/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9171</ref>
==Overview== [[Image:Japanese Primordial Deities-ja.svg|thumb|right|200px|{{ill|Creation of Heaven and Earth|ja|天地開闢|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Creation of Heaven and Earth|1=Q11442446|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11442446}} {{ill|Japanese Primordial Deities (SVG)|ja|メディア:Japanese Primordial Deities-ja.svg|lt=SVG display (compatible browsers only)|lt_ja=SVGで表示（対応ブラウザのみ）|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Primordial Deities (SVG)|qq=}}]]
'''Omotaru''' (淤母陀琉, also known as Menashison) ・ '''Ayakashikone'''／'''Ayakashikine''' (阿夜訶志古泥, also known as Kyoukonson) are deities appearing in {{ill|Japanese mythology|en|Japanese mythology|WD=Q814158|lt=Japanese Mythology|1=Japanese mythology}}. {{Needs verification|{{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Jinmu Tennō|1=Emperor Jimmu}} is his great-grandson|date=February 2025}}
== Overview == In the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki|1=Kojiki}}, the elder sibling is presented as '''Omotaru-no-Kami''' and the younger as '''Ayakashikone-no-Kami'''; in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}, the elder is rendered as '''Menashison''' and the younger as '''Ayakashikone''' (Ayakashikine).
In the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki|1=Kojiki}}, they are regarded as the sixth-generation deities of the {{ill|Kamiyonanayo|en|Kamiyonanayo|WD=Q3275893|lt=Kamiyonanayo|1=Kamiyonanayo}}, with the elder, Omotaru-no-Kami, being a male deity and the younger, Ayakashikone-no-Kami, a female deity. The name "Omotaru" means "completed (i.e., without deficiency)," while "Ayakashikone" is an honorific epithet derived from "ayanikashi"—thus, they deify the perfection of the human body. {{Sfn|Kurano|2007|p=20}}{{Sfn|Nakayama|1999|p=59}}
Furthermore, Omotaru-no-Kami is interpreted by reading "淤母" as "face" and "陀琉" as "to suffice," thereby naming him to denote "a man whose facial appearance is completely satisfying." In light of the contextual correspondence with Ayakashikone-no-Kami and the enduring practice of phallic worship, his naming is also seen as a praise of the male genital form. Similarly, Ayakashikone-no-Kami is parsed with "阿夜" as an interjection, "訶志古" as the stem of "to fear," and "泥" as an honorific for persons; hence, her name implies "Oh, most venerable woman," symbolizing the awe inspired by the potent spiritual power of the female genitalia. {{Sfn|Nishimiya|2014|pp=325–326}}
In the {{ill|Middle Ages|en|Middle Ages|WD=Q12554|lt=Medieval Period|1=Middle Ages}}, through the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism ({{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|en|Shinbutsu-shūgō|WD=Q2279088|lt=Shinbutsu-shūgō|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}}), these deities—as the sixth-generation in the {{ill|Kamiyonanayo|en|Kamiyonanayo|WD=Q3275893|lt=Kamiyonanayo|1=Kamiyonanayo}}—came to be regarded as the manifestation ({{ill|Suijaku|ja|垂迹|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suijaku|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 本地垂迹|1=Honji suijaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3080781}}) of the {{ill|Demon King of the Sixth Heaven|lt=Sixth Heavenly Demon King|ja|天魔|1=Demon King of the Sixth Heaven|lt=Demon King of the Sixth Heaven|1=Demon King of the Sixth Heaven|qq=Sixth Heavenly Demon King|lt_ja=第六天魔王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Demon King of the Sixth Heaven|13=User:Immanuelle/Demon King of the Sixth Heaven|qq=}}, the highest Rank of the {{ill|six heavens of the desire realm|ja|六欲天|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/six heavens of the desire realm|qq=}} in the Buddhist desire realm. They were especially venerated in {{ill|Shugendō|en|Shugendō|WD=Q1483875|lt=Shugendō|1=Shugendō}}. With the separation of Shinto and Buddhism in the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} ({{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|WD=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu Bunri|1=Shinbutsu bunri}}), many temples ({{ill|Buddhist Temple|ja|寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhist Temple|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 寺院|1=Buddhist temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5393308}}) enshrining the Sixth Heavenly Demon King were converted into shrines ({{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto Shrine|1=Shinto shrine}}), and were renamed as, for example, {{ill|Dairokuten Shrine|lt=Dairokuten Shrines|ja|第六天神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dairokuten Shrine|qq=}}, Koro Shrine, and {{ill|Menashi Shrine|ja|面足神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Menashi Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 第六天神社|1=Dairokuten Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11602399}}.

Deities-eng.svg|frame]] {{ill|Omodaru and Ayakashikone|ja|オモダル・アヤカシコネ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omodaru and Ayakashikone}} is a pair of Japanese primordial deities<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Aragona |first=Jared |title=from Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Vol. 1 |url=https://open.maricopa.edu/worldmythologyvolume1godsandcreation/chapter/from-nihongi-chronicles-of-japan-from-the-earliest-times-to-a-d-697-vol-1/ |language=en}}</ref><ref name="heavens">{{Cite web |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 1 |url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697/Book_I |via=Wikisource}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=2021-04-11 |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Omodaru, Ayakashikone |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=124 |access-date=2023-03-22 |archive-url=http://web.archive.org/web/20210411072506/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=124 |archive-date=2021-04-11 }}</ref><ref>https://archive.ph/a8w1z</ref>
They were preceded by {{ill|Otonoji and Otonobe|ja|オオトノヂ・オオトノベ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otonoji and Otonobe|qq=}} and succeeded by [[Izanagi]] and {{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami|1=Izanami}}.
In medieval times they were identified with the {{ill|Demon King of the Sixth Heaven|lt=Demon King of the Sixth Heaven|ja|天魔|lt_ja=第六天魔王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Demon King of the Sixth Heaven}} <ref>https://web.archive.org/web/20221225101550/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9171</ref>
==Overview== [[Image:Japanese Primordial Deities-ja.svg|thumb|right|200px|{{ill|Creation of Heaven and Earth|ja|天地開闢|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Creation of Heaven and Earth|1=Q11442446|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11442446}} {{ill|Japanese Primordial Deities (SVG)|ja|メディア:Japanese Primordial Deities-ja.svg|lt=SVG display (compatible browsers only)|lt_ja=SVGで表示（対応ブラウザのみ）|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Primordial Deities (SVG)|qq=}}]]
'''Omotaru''' (淤母陀琉, also known as Menashison) ・ '''Ayakashikone'''／'''Ayakashikine''' (阿夜訶志古泥, also known as Kyoukonson) are deities appearing in {{ill|Japanese mythology|en|Japanese mythology|WD=Q814158|lt=Japanese Mythology|1=Japanese mythology}}. {{Needs verification|{{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Jinmu Tennō|1=Emperor Jimmu}} is his great-grandson|date=February 2025}}
== Overview == In the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki|1=Kojiki}}, the elder sibling is presented as '''Omotaru-no-Kami''' and the younger as '''Ayakashikone-no-Kami'''; in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}, the elder is rendered as '''Menashison''' and the younger as '''Ayakashikone''' (Ayakashikine).
In the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki|1=Kojiki}}, they are regarded as the sixth-generation deities of the {{ill|Kamiyonanayo|en|Kamiyonanayo|WD=Q3275893|lt=Kamiyonanayo|1=Kamiyonanayo}}, with the elder, Omotaru-no-Kami, being a male deity and the younger, Ayakashikone-no-Kami, a female deity. The name "Omotaru" means "completed (i.e., without deficiency)," while "Ayakashikone" is an honorific epithet derived from "ayanikashi"—thus, they deify the perfection of the human body. {{Sfn|Kurano|2007|p=20}}{{Sfn|Nakayama|1999|p=59}}
Furthermore, Omotaru-no-Kami is interpreted by reading "淤母" as "face" and "陀琉" as "to suffice," thereby naming him to denote "a man whose facial appearance is completely satisfying." In light of the contextual correspondence with Ayakashikone-no-Kami and the enduring practice of phallic worship, his naming is also seen as a praise of the male genital form. Similarly, Ayakashikone-no-Kami is parsed with "阿夜" as an interjection, "訶志古" as the stem of "to fear," and "泥" as an honorific for persons; hence, her name implies "Oh, most venerable woman," symbolizing the awe inspired by the potent spiritual power of the female genitalia. {{Sfn|Nishimiya|2014|pp=325–326}}
In the {{ill|Middle Ages|en|Middle Ages|WD=Q12554|lt=Medieval Period|1=Middle Ages}}, through the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism ({{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|en|Shinbutsu-shūgō|WD=Q2279088|lt=Shinbutsu-shūgō|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}}), these deities—as the sixth-generation in the {{ill|Kamiyonanayo|en|Kamiyonanayo|WD=Q3275893|lt=Kamiyonanayo|1=Kamiyonanayo}}—came to be regarded as the manifestation ({{ill|Suijaku|ja|垂迹|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suijaku|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 本地垂迹|1=Honji suijaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3080781}}) of the {{ill|Demon King of the Sixth Heaven|lt=Sixth Heavenly Demon King|ja|天魔|1=Demon King of the Sixth Heaven|lt=Demon King of the Sixth Heaven|1=Demon King of the Sixth Heaven|qq=Sixth Heavenly Demon King|lt_ja=第六天魔王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Demon King of the Sixth Heaven|13=User:Immanuelle/Demon King of the Sixth Heaven|qq=}}, the highest Rank of the {{ill|six heavens of the desire realm|ja|六欲天|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/six heavens of the desire realm|qq=}} in the Buddhist desire realm. They were especially venerated in {{ill|Shugendō|en|Shugendō|WD=Q1483875|lt=Shugendō|1=Shugendō}}. With the separation of Shinto and Buddhism in the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} ({{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|WD=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu Bunri|1=Shinbutsu bunri}}), many temples ({{ill|Buddhist Temple|ja|寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhist Temple|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 寺院|1=Buddhist temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5393308}}) enshrining the Sixth Heavenly Demon King were converted into shrines ({{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto Shrine|1=Shinto shrine}}), and were renamed as, for example, {{ill|Dairokuten Shrine|lt=Dairokuten Shrines|ja|第六天神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dairokuten Shrine|qq=}}, Koro Shrine, and {{ill|Menashi Shrine|ja|面足神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Menashi Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 第六天神社|1=Dairokuten Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11602399}}.
== Names == {|border="1" cellspacing="0" cellpadding="10" |-bgcolor="#EEEEEE" ! Source !! Group 1 !! Group 2 !! Group 3 !! Group 4 !! Group 5 !! Group 6 !! Group 7 !! Group 8 !!
